coredata.dc:title: A functional ratio fluorescence sensor platform based on the graphene/Mn-ZnS quantum dots loaded with molecularly imprinted polymer for selective and visual detection sinapic acid

coredata.dc:description: A functional ratio fluorescence sensor based on the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coated double quantum dots (QDs) being composited of Mn-ZnS QDs and silica-coated graphene quantum dots (GQDs@SiO2) had been established for the sensitive, selective and visual detection of sinapic acid (SA). MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was synthesized through a simple one-pot sol-gel reaction, and it exhibited two fluorescence emission peaks with yellow fluorescence of Mn-ZnS QDs at 580 nm and the blue fluorescence of GQDs at 445 nm. SA can selectively enhance the fluorescence of GQDs but quench the Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence to the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2. The ratio of fluorescence enhancement to fluorescence reduction is linear with the concentration of SA from 9 to 81 nM with the detection limits of 0.8388 nM (S/N = 3). And the constructed fluorescent probe can also be used to visually detect SA according to the change of color. More importantly, molecular imprinting technique enables the sensors to selectively recognize the SA while other similar structure molecules hardly interfere with the SA determination in the measurement environment. Meanwhile, the fluorescence sensors have the advantages of fast response time and long duration of fluorescence intensity. These excellent performances made the proposed method to be applied for the determination of SA in Semen Sinapis and Descurainiae Semen.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A functional ratio fluorescence sensor based on the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coated double quantum dots (QDs) being composited of Mn-ZnS QDs and silica-coated graphene quantum dots (GQDs@SiO2) had been established for the sensitive, selective and visual detection of sinapic acid (SA). MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was synthesized through a simple one-pot sol-gel reaction, and it exhibited two fluorescence emission peaks with yellow fluorescence of Mn-ZnS QDs at 580 nm and the blue fluorescence of GQDs at 445 nm. SA can selectively enhance the fluorescence of GQDs but quench the Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence to the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2. The ratio of fluorescence enhancement to fluorescence reduction is linear with the concentration of SA from 9 to 81 nM with the detection limits of 0.8388 nM (S/N = 3). And the constructed fluorescent probe can also be used to visually detect SA according to the change of color. More importantly, molecular imprinting technique enables the sensors to selectively recognize the SA while other similar structure molecules hardly interfere with the SA determination in the measurement environment. Meanwhile, the fluorescence sensors have the advantages of fast response time and long duration of fluorescence intensity. These excellent performances made the proposed method to be applied for the determination of SA in Semen Sinapis and Descurainiae Semen.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic acids are commonly used as medicinal product, pharmacological materials and important food additives of functional foods due to their highly efficient biological activity [1]. Sinapic acid (SA), a natural phenolic acid, is usually found in fruits, crops, and Chinese herbal medicines such as dioscorea opposite thumb, Citrus reticulata Blanco, Descurainiae Semen, and Semen Sinapis [2,3]. It is considered to possess a variety of medicinal activities, such as antioxidant [4], anti-inflammatory [5], anti-bacterial [6], anti-cancer [7] and anti-anxiety [8]. So SA has been recommended for the food processing, cosmetics and drug synthesis [9,10]. However, clear evidence is pointing out that the adverse effects can be mechanistically caused by high-dose polyphenols through so-called pro-oxidative action [1]. So far, high performance liquid chromatography [11,12], thin layer chromatography [13], electrochemical methods [14] and spectrophotometry [15] have been reported to detect SA. In spite of their reliability and convenience, their costly equipment, complex sample preprocessing and low selectivity have limited their extensive use.

In recent years, fluorescence analysis had been attracted wide attention with its low cost, convenience and high sensitivity [16–19]. Quantum dots (QDs) are a new type of fluorescent nanomaterials and they had been widely applied in fluorescence sensors because of their good characteristics performance including distinguished chemical and optical stability, homogeneous nanoparticles obtained by modification, excellent selectivity, wider excitation spectrum, narrow symmetrical emission peaks, high fluorescence productivity and lower noise effect [20–22]. However, QDs sometimes can response to several species for the simultaneous determination [23]. Thus enhancing the selectivity of quantum dots is the key factor for improving the overall performance of quantum dots. Fortunately, the surface modification of QDs with specific groups or materials can improve the selectivity [24].

Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) is one of the methods to improve the poor selectivity of QDs. It can obtain the pre-designed specific binding sites by eluting the template molecule to form many cavities on the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) that matches the size of the target template [25–27]. The MIPs principally exhibit the static force, shape and functional action for the target molecules so they have been diffusely used in bioinstrumentation, sensors, dissociation, catalysis and other fields [28–30]. MIPs fluorescence sensor can precisely identify the target molecules, leading to the change of fluorescence intensity. The intensity of fluorescence quenching or enhancement can be used for quantification [18,31,32]. In the presence of the template, MIPs are generated by the copolymerization of functional monomers and cross linking agent through various methods such as bulk polymerization, sol-gel polymerization, precipitation polymerization, and suspension polymerization [33,34]. The sol-gel method (e.g. Stӧber method or reverse micro-emulsion method) is one of the most popular synthetic strategies at present because of its simple preparation and excellent water solubility.

In light of that, a ratio fluorescent molecular imprinting sensor has been designed based on double quantum dots (Mn-ZnS quantum dots and the SiO2 coated graphene quantum dots) molecularly imprinted polymer (MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2) for the fluorescence detection and colorimetric detection of SA. We prepared the blue GQDs by heating the citric acid, and the orange Mn-ZnS QDs were obtained by simple one pot method with 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) side sealing. Subsequently, the molecularly imprinted polymer containing double quantum dots was synthesized with SA as the template molecule. Interestingly, SA could selectively enhance the fluorescence of GQDs but cause the Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence quenching, the ratio of fluorescence enhancement to fluorescence decrease is linear with the concentration of SA, so MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 use the two quantum dots as detection units and the MIP as recognition units. And the constructed fluorescent probe can also be used to visually detect SA according to the change of color. In addition, the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor had been studied. Finally, the application of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 in the detection of SA in Chinese herbal medicine was also investigated.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) (98%), ZnSO4·7H2O (99.5%), MnSO4·H2O (99.0%), H3PO4 (99%), Na2S·9H2O (98%), acetic acid (99.5%), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), NH3·H2O (25%), sinapic acid (SA), chlorogenic acid (CA), oleanonic acid (OA), vanillic acid (VA), trans-cinnamic acid (TCA) and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and received without further purification. The water supplied in the experimental procedures was double distilled water (DDW).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100HR, JEOL) was applied to record the morphologies of the observed materials. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (EVO18, Zeiss, Germany) were used to observe the morphologies and element analysis of the materials. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum with a D/max 2500 (18KW) X-Ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) was performed to characterize the nanoparticle sizes distribution. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Infrared spectra were measured on a Spectrum 100 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The UV–vis adsorption spectra of the materials were measured on a UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) UV–vis spectrophotometer. Time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out using a time correlated single-photon counting (FLS920P, Edinburgh Instruments) lifetime spectroscopy system with a picosecond pulsed diode laser (EPL-350 nm) as the single wavelength excitation light source.

GQDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of citric acid according to the method of reference [35] with minor modification. Briefly, citric acid (2.0 g) was heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle until the liquid became pale yellow. Subsequently, 100 mL of 0.25 M sodium hydroxide solution was added dropwise into the yellow solution and stirred for 30 min. Then the obtained solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (14 kDa cut off) for 24 h. At last, the synthesized GQD nanoparticles were stored in solution at 4 °C until use. While GQDs@SiO2 was synthesized by sol-gel process according to the reported method with some modifications [36]. Firstly, 5 mmol of APTES were added into 20 mL of GQDs solution under vigorous stirring and heated at 40 °C for 30 min to obtain the APTES-coated GQDs. Secondly, 10.0 mL of APTES-coated GQDs, 2.0 mL of APTES, 1.0 mL of TEOS and 2.5 mL of NH3·H2O were added into the ethanol solution and stirred for 12 h. Finally, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and DDW and dried in vacuum at 50 °C.

According to the previous report [37], the method of the synthesis of Mn-ZnS quantum dots had been slightly modified. First, 10 mL of ZnSO4 solution (0.1 M), 2 mL of MnCl2 solution (0.01 M) and 355 μL of MPA were sequentially added to a three-necked round bottom flask and stirred for 10 min until these solutions were completely mixed. Then the pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 11 with 1 M NaOH. After that, the solution was stirred in N2 for 30 min, and then quickly injected 5 mL of Na2S solution (0.1 M) with a syringe. The mixed solution continued to be stirred in N2 for 30 min. Then, the original solution was opened and placed in air at 50 °C for 3 h. Afterwards an equal amount of absolute ethanol was added to precipitate QDs, and the supernatant was discarded by centrifugation. Finally, the product was washed with absolute ethanol for three times, and dried in vacuum for 12 h.

For the preparation of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2, the sol-gel method was applied again according to the reference [38]. Under ultrasonic conditions, 9.8 mg of Mn-ZnS QDs and 100 mL of APTES were mixed with 50 mL ethanol and stirred for 6 h. Then 50 mg of GQDs@SiO2, 10 mg of SA and 100 μL of TEOS were dispersed into the above solutions with drastic agitation. After 30 min, the reaction was initiated with the addition of 250 μL of NH3·H2O and the solutions were stirred for 24 h at room temperature. Finally, the product was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol for three times and dried in vacuum at 50 °C. The template SA was extracted through elution with acetic acid and methanol (1:9, v/v) by using Soxhlet extractor. As a contrast, the non-imprinted polymers (NIP@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2) were fabricated by the same procedure but without addition of SA and the imprinted polymers (MIP@Mn-ZnS) were fabricated by the same procedure but without addition of GQDs@SiO2. The preparation of the ratiometric fluorescence sensors MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Fluorescence detection experiments were conducted with both excitation and emission slit widths of 10 nm, the excitation wavelength of 310 nm and the recorded emission wavelength range was 350–750 nm. For the detection of SA, in the Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing 1 mg/mL of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2, 10 μL of SA solutions with different concentrations were added in it. After incubation for 3 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded, and then the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 445 nm and 580 nm (F 445/F 580) was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SA in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: Semen Sinapis and Descurainiae Semen (Northwest Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd.) were bought from a local supermarket in Guangzhou and they were used as samples to investigate the potential application of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2. A certain amount of Semen Sinapis or Descurainiae Semen were powdered and screened, and the produced powder was dissolved in methanol for reflux extraction for 1 h, and then filtered to obtain the sample solution. For fluorescence detection, some drug solutions were mixed with 5.0 mL of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 in B-R buffer solution (the concentration of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was 1 mg/mL) at pH 7.0 for about 3 min, and then subjected to fluorescence detection. The samples were also spiked with different concentrations of SA standard solution to evaluate the detection method.

Fig. 1 showed the TEM and SEM of prepared materials. The uniform distribution of GQDs exhibited a uniformly sized sphere as could be seen in the TEM of Fig. 1a. We could see from the particle size distribution graph (insertion in Fig. 1a) that the particle size range of GQDs was between 4.4 and 5.6 nm. And Mn-ZnS QDs also showed a uniform spherical shape but the particle sizes of Mn-ZnS QDs were ranged from 5.6 nm to 6.8 nm (insertion in Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c presented a uniform spherical shape polymerized together and the diameter of the ball was about 52 nm, and it revealed that GQDs were successfully coated by silica layer. After the surface was imprinted and polymerized, we could found the apparent variations (Fig. 1d) that the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 formed the larger complexes with the particle size of 165 ± 5.0 nm. These indicated that the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 were prepared successfully via sol-gel polymerization.

With the help of Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet spectroscopy (UV), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), we knew more clearly that the element composition and functional group structure of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs @SiO2. The FT-IR spectrogram of GQDs, GQDs@SiO2, MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and NIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was shown in Fig. 2a. It could be seen from Fig. 2a, four peaks at the band of 3398 cm−1, 1450 cm−1, 1660 cm−1 and 2934 cm−1 are in all four QDs, of which the broadband at 3398 cm−1 is due to the stretching of -OH, and a peak at 1450 cm−1 corresponds to skeletal vibration of aromatic rings. The stretching vibrations of CO and CH are located at 1660 cm−1 and 2934 cm−1, respectively [35,39]. The peak at 617 cm−1 can be seen in MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs @SiO2 (blue line) and NIPs@ Mn-ZnS/GQDs @SiO2 (red line), which was assigned to the ZnS band corresponding to sulfides. In the meantime, the stretching vibrations of SiO and Si-O-Si were located at 790 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1, respectively, which were generated from TEOS [40], suggesting that the SiO2 was successfully grafted onto the surface of the GQDs, MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs @SiO2 and NIPs@ Mn-ZnS/GQDs @SiO2. Moreover, the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs @SiO2 and NIPs@ Mn-ZnS/GQDs @SiO2 were displayed similar absorption peaks position and strength, the results testified that the template of SA was removed absolutely. The UV absorption spectrum of Fig. 2b has revealed the template was eluted completely by eluting the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 for three times. The graph (Fig. 2c, blue curve) showed us that the crystal lattice of (111), (220), and (311) produced corresponding broad peaks at 2θ value of 28.0°, 48.0° and 56.0°, respectively. It was indicated that the nanocrystals illustrated peak locations of the cube closed packed structure of zinc blend [37]. And a broad peak (Fig. 2c, red curve) at about 26.5° was (002) plane, diameter estimated of 0.342 nm, suggesting that in the process of synthesizing GQDs, the carbon content of GQDs increases and there are some graphite structures in it [35].

In the EDS spectrum (Fig. 2d), the peak value of 2.3 keV caused by S element, and the peaks at 1.0 keV and 5.9 keV could be designated as Zn and Mn, respectively, indicating the presence of Mn-ZnS. The peak at 0.25 keV caused by C indicated the presence of GQDs. In addition, we could see the peaks of about 1.7 keV and 0.45 keV caused by Si and O, respectively, indicating the presence of SiO2.
The results of FT-IR, UV, XRD and EDS were further illustrated that the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 had been synthesized favourably.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SA fluorescence recognition and mechanism based on the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the fluorescence recognition mechanism of the prepared sensors, we first studied the fluorescence characteristics of GQDs, GQDs over SA, Mn-ZnS QDs and Mn-ZnS QDs over SA (see Supporting information). It can be seen from the Fig. S1, Mn-ZnS QDs appears two fluorescence peaks (blue line) at about 445 nm for ZnS QDs and 580 nm for Mn-ZnS QDs, the fluorescence peak at 580 nm is due to the interaction of Mn2+ with ZnS, thus Mn2+ has a forbidden transition of 4T1-6A1 [41–43], while the fluorescence peak at about 445 nm is attributed to the defects of ZnS [43]. When SA was added to the solution with Mn-ZnS QDs, the fluorescence peak intensity at 580 nm had decreased, but at 445 nm had increased (green line). Only one fluorescence peak is located at 450 nm when SA is added to the GQDs solution (red line), the position and intensity of fluorescence peak were almost consistent with that of GQDs (black line). It can be concluded that Mn-ZnS QDs is sensitive to SA both at 445 nm and 580 nm, while GQDs had little response to SA. In this experiment, the prepared GQDs were encapsulated with silica layer, while silica layers have some performances such as rigid structure, chemically inert and hydrophilic capability [44]. It has been reported that GQDs coated with SiO2 can avoid contacting with external drugs and serve as an internal standard in the composite materials [45]. So GQDs is insensitive to SA.

In order to investigate the fluorescence recognition mechanism of the prepared sensors, we first studied the fluorescence characteristics of GQDs, GQDs over SA, Mn-ZnS QDs and Mn-ZnS QDs over SA (see Supporting information). It can be seen from the Fig. S1, Mn-ZnS QDs appears two fluorescence peaks (blue line) at about 445 nm for ZnS QDs and 580 nm for Mn-ZnS QDs, the fluorescence peak at 580 nm is due to the interaction of Mn2+ with ZnS, thus Mn2+ has a forbidden transition of 4T1-6A1 [41–43], while the fluorescence peak at about 445 nm is attributed to the defects of ZnS [43]. When SA was added to the solution with Mn-ZnS QDs, the fluorescence peak intensity at 580 nm had decreased, but at 445 nm had increased (green line). Only one fluorescence peak is located at 450 nm when SA is added to the GQDs solution (red line), the position and intensity of fluorescence peak were almost consistent with that of GQDs (black line). It can be concluded that Mn-ZnS QDs is sensitive to SA both at 445 nm and 580 nm, while GQDs had little response to SA. In this experiment, the prepared GQDs were encapsulated with silica layer, while silica layers have some performances such as rigid structure, chemically inert and hydrophilic capability [44]. It has been reported that GQDs coated with SiO2 can avoid contacting with external drugs and serve as an internal standard in the composite materials [45]. So GQDs is insensitive to SA.

It can be seen from Fig. 3a, The fluorescent composite MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 (blue line) and NIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 (black line) displayed two fluorescent peaks with different wavelengths at 445 nm and 580 nm under the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. When SA was added to the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 solution, the fluorescence of GQDs at 445 nm was enhanced but fluorescence decay of Mn-ZnS QDs was at 580 nm. The enhancement of the fluorescence of GQDs may be that the metal elements of Mn-ZnS QDs interact with SA to form complex, which enhance the rigidity of SA and enhance the fluorescence of SA. Meanwhile, the GQDs which are not completely wrapped by SiO2 are planar two-dimensional structure, and then may be form a larger rigid structure with metal SA complex, which makes GQDs fluorescence increase.

To investigate the mechanism of SA-mediated Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence quenching (static or dynamic quenching mechanism), MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2@SA were performed for fluorescence lifetime detection (Fig. 3b). Time-resolved PL measurements showed that fluorescence lifetime of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2@SA fluorescence quenching is 10.05 ns and 5.70 ns, respectively. According to the Reference [46], the mechanism of SA-mediated Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence quenching was dynamic quenching mechanism. And it could be concluded that the interaction between SA anion and surface atoms of Mn-ZnS QDs blocks the electron-hole pair recombination [41], the fluorescence quenching in this experiment was due to charge transfer from MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 to SA.

The Scheme 1 demonstrated the SA detection and recognition performance with MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 ratio fluorescence sensor to visually analyze the SA. At the excitation wavelength of 310 nm, when MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was combined with the target SA, the fluorescence intensity was increased at 445 nm, and it was relatively weakened at 580 nm (Fig. 3a, red line). After the target SA was removed, the intensity of the fluorescence at 445 nm began to decrease, while the fluorescence intensity at 580 nm gradually recovered to the same as that of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 (Fig. 3a, blue line) and the similar to that of NIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 (Fig. 3a, black line). Accordingly, MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 underwent a noticeable fluorescence color change from light blue to orange, which was similar to the color of NIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 under UV light. This phenomenon could indicate that MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 had an excellent recognition effect on SA and could be used to analyze SA with naked eyes under UV light.

To obtain a broad linear range with a high sensitivity and short analysis time in the quantitative determination of SA, the experimental conditions (e.g. the synthesis conditions of Mn-ZnS QDs, pH, and response time) of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 were investigated and optimized.

In order to investigate whether Mn concentration affected the fluorescence intensity of quantum dots, Mn-ZnS QDs were synthesized with different Mn concentrations and other conditions were kept the same. Fig. 4a (black line) showed that the fluorescence effect was optimal at the concentration of 0.010 mol/L Mn2+. At the excitation wavelength of 310 nm, the fluorescence intensity tended to decrease with the increase of Mn2+ concentration. It was speculated that the possible reason for the decrease of the fluorescence intensity was that the increase of Mn2+ concentration further exacerbated QDs clusters or complexation, thus the phenomenon of self-quenching was appeared [47]. Therefore, 0.010 mol/L Mn2+ is selected as the optimal concentration for Mn-ZnS quantum dots synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimize the pH of synthetic Mn-ZnS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of Mn-ZnS quantum dot is affected by the pH in the synthesis process. The pH of the reaction system is adjusted by changing the amount of NaOH added. It could be seen from Fig. 4b that the fluorescence intensity at 580 nm reached the best when the pH value is 11. The results were consistent with the literature reports [37]. This is because the decrease of pH breaks the balance between the H+, S2−, HS− and H2S and leads to the decrease of S2− in the solution, which caused the fluorescence disappearance of QDs [37]. So pH 11 is selected as the optimal synthesis pH condition for Mn-ZnS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimize the pH of MIPs and MIPs with SA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and the interactions between SA and MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 were directly affected by the pH value of the solution. Therefore, we selected pH values between 2.0 and 12.0 to study their effects on the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 with or without SA (Fig. 4c). It can be seen that the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 showed the best and stable fluorescence intensity at pH 6.0–8.0, while the highest quenching efficiency was deduced at pH 6.0–8.0 for MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2@SA. It is reported that SA had two pKa values of 4.47 and 9.21, respectively, so it presents three ionic forms of cation, anion and divalent anion in the pH range from 2.0 to 12.0, and all the three ionic forms of SA have fluorescence at about 450 nm [48]. When the pH value was below 6.0, the fluorescence of Mn-ZnS QDs was inhibited because the Mn-ZnS QDs were synthesized under alkaline conditions [17]. Meanwhile, SA exists mainly in cation form (pH < 4.47), in cation and anion form (4.47 < pH < 6). When MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 interacted with SA, Mn2+ and Zn2+ quenched the SA cation form fluorescence, while increased the SA anion form fluorescence by the coordination with SA anion form. Therefore, the ration of fluorescence intensity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 increased with SA anion form increased gradually from pH 2 to 6. Under alkaline conditions (pH > 8), for one thing, the molecularly imprinted surface is destroyed due to the corrosion of alkaline conditions [49], for another, SA mainly exists in anion and divalent anion form (8 < pH < 9.21), and divalent anion form (pH > 9.21), SA with the negative charge and the negatively charged quantum dot can produce electrostatic repulsion effect to reduce the combination of SA and MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 with the increase of pH, thus fluorescence intensity was decreased. When the pH value is in the range of 6–8, SA is mainly in anion and divalent anion form, the interaction between SA anion and surface atoms of Mn-ZnS QDs blocks the electron-hole pair recombination, then fluorescence quenching is observed at 580 nm. However, surface atoms of Mn-ZnS QDs can interact with SA anion to form complex, which increases the plane rigid structure of SA and enhances the fluorescence of SA at about 445 nm. Meanwhile, graphene quantum dots are two-dimensional planar structures, and the GQDs not completely wrapped by SiO2 can interact with metal-SA complex to form a larger rigid structure, which also increases the fluorescence at about 445 nm. The maximum difference of fluorescence intensity between MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 with SA was at pH 7.0, so that Britton-Robinson (pH 7.0) was used for further experiments.

Whether the fluorescence intensity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 is stable or not is another important factor to evaluate the quality of the sensor. So we studied the stability of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and repeatedly tested its fluorescence intensity for 600 min and 30 days at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 4d and e, the fluorescence intensity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was not quenched basically during the first 600 min and its quenching efficiency was less than 8.54%. However, the fluorescence intensity after 30 days was still relatively stable but the fluorescence intensity decreased to 78.05% of the initial intensity. The experimental result indicated that the customized MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 could protect Mn-ZnS QDs and GQDs from external factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incubation time of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 to SA
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 4f, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor decreased quickly at 580 nm during the first 3 min after SA was added, and then it decreased slowly and remained stable from 3 min to 20 min. Thus, we chose the incubation time was 3 min. The short incubation time also indicated that MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 could apply to the rapid analysis of SA in the solution.

Sensitivity is an important condition to evaluate the analytical method. Under the same conditions, the sensitivity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2, NIPs@ Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and MIPs@Mn-ZnS was evaluated by adding the same amount of SA. As we could see from the Fig. 5a, when the concentration of SA (9–81 nM) increased, the fluorescence intensity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 at 580 nm was continuously quenched with certain regularity, but the fluorescence intensity near 445 nm was gradually enhanced (Fig. 5a, from green curve to black curve). When the same amount of SA was added, the fluorescence intensity of NIPs@ Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 at 580 nm began to decline slowly, but gradually increased at 445 nm (Fig. 5b, from black curve to pink curve). Correspondingly, under UV light, the relative fluorescence of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 had a more obvious color change from orange to blue (insertion in Fig. 5a) than that of NIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 (insertion in Fig. 5b). This phenomenon was mainly caused by many recognition sites in MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2, which could be selectively and quickly matched with SA after embossing process. As shown in Fig. 5c, when the SA was added, the fluorescence of MIPs@Mn-ZnS was quenched at 580 nm, and was slightly enhanced at 445 nm because the SA itself had photoluminescence characteristics. However, the color change (insertion in Fig. 5c) was not so obvious that it was difficult to observe with naked eyes under the ultraviolet lamp when detecting SA with MIPs@Mn-ZnS. Therefore, the results showed that the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was more suitable for visual detection of SA.

Because the ratiometric fluorescence probe MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 is more sensitive than NIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and MIPs@Mn-ZnS, so MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was used for the quantitative analysis of SA. Based on the ratio of fluorescence intensity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 between 445 nm and 580 nm ((F445/F580)0), we investigated the change of the ratio of fluorescence intensity after adding SA. As shown in Fig. 5d, the value of log ((F445/F580)/(F445/F580)0) was enhanced slowly with the additions of SA from 9 to 81 nM, and the linear equation was log ((F445/F580)/(F445/F580)0) = 0.009 × CSA(nM) − 0.106 (R2 = 0.9930) with the detection limit of 0.8388 nM (S/N = 3). In consequence, the sensor MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 provided high sensitivity and a wide linear range when it is used for the instant detection of SA.

The selectivity of the sensor is another important factor for accurately analyze the target molecule. We conducted a controlled trial on the sensor to evaluate the selective performance of the sensor MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2. It can be seen from Fig. 6a, when there were 3 μM different kinds of ions (including HCO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, H2PO4 −, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+ and Fe3+) coexisted with MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2, the sensor had been slightly quenched by Ag+ and Mn2+ (Fig. 6a, red column), but there was no significant change in the determination of SA when the above ions coexist with MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 (Fig. 6a, blue column). The selectivity of QDs in the presence of drugs with similar structure to SA including CA, VA, OA, TCA and LA was shown in Fig. 6b. It can be seen that the ratio of fluorescence intensity of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 and NIPs @Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 had only slightly changed in the presence of CA, VA, OA, TCA and LA, while SA had an obviously quenching effect on MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 but had small inhibition of NIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2. Through these experiment results, we learned that MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 was highly selective and could accurately identify SA in complex environments.

We applied the sensor MIPs @Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 to the quantitative analysis of SA in Semen Sinapis and Descurainiae Semen, respectively. No sample pretreatment process was requested except for filtration and extraction. SA was estimated in Semen Sinapis and Descurainiae Semen at concentrations of 0.0502–0.0513% and 0.0416–0.0413%, respectively. The recovery of SA was between 99.07 and 103.0%, and the RSD value was between 0.28 and 4.7%, the detailed results were shown in Table 1 .

Fluorescence has been used to study SA for a long time. Smyk group [48] studied the three ionic forms and the fluorescence characteristics of SA in aqueous solution. Subsequently, the interaction of SA with bovine serum albumin and egg protein and human serum albumin by fluorescence was reported [50,51]. And the effect of SA on the interaction between Poziotinib and bovine serum albumin by fluorescence was also reported [52]. However, these methods do not give analysis characteristics such as linear range and detection limit. Various determination methods of SA have been compared with the prepared methods, which are listed in Table 2 .

As shown in Table 2, our detection system was more sensitive than previously reported methods. It may be the reason that the prepared method is a ratio fluorescence sensor, the sensor can be applied to quantitatively detect SA according to the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of two QDs, and so the sensitivity of this method is increased. Meanwhile, the proposed proportional fluorescence sensor based on MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 can be used to the semi-quantitative determination of SA with naked eyes according to the color change under UV lamp. Therefore, the prepared method is effective and sensitive.

Summarily, we had successfully prepared a new ratio fluorescence sensor based on the MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2. The sensor presented two fluorescence emission peaks with yellow fluorescence of Mn-ZnS QDs at 580 nm and the blue fluorescence of GQDs at 445 nm. SA can mediate Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence quenching and make GQDs fluorescence enhancement when it is added to the B-R solution with MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2. The results with time-resolved PL measurements show that SA-mediated Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence quenching was dynamic quenching mechanism. It may be the reason that the interaction between SA anion and surface atoms of Mn-ZnS QDs blocks the electron-hole pair recombination, and the electron transferred from MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 to SA, thus making Mn-ZnS QDs fluorescence quenching. The interaction between metal ions, SA and GQDs increases the planar rigid structure, which may be the reason for the fluorescence enhancement of GQDs. The ratio of fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence quenching of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2 is proportional to the concentration of SA, which had been successfully determined SA in actual sample by fluorescence method. Moreover, different concentrations of SA can change the color of MIPs@Mn-ZnS/GQDs@SiO2, which makes the sensor to be used to semi-quantitatively determine SA with naked eyes. The strategy of our prepared ratio fluorescence sensor is expected to provide an important detection platform for other phenolic acids.
